pathways.label: General arrest, charge, plea, trial, or conviction expungement under RSMo § 610.140
pathways.summary: Section 610.140 allows a person to apply in the Missouri court where they were charged or found guilty for expungement of records of arrest, plea, trial, or conviction, so long as the crime occurred in Missouri, was prosecuted under Missouri court jurisdiction, is listed in the petition, and is not excluded by statute. ruleClauses: Section 610.140 allows a person to apply in the Missouri court where they were charged or found guilty for expungement of records of arrest, plea, trial, or conviction, so long as the crime occurred in Missouri, was prosecuted under Missouri court jurisdiction, is listed in the petition, and is not excluded by statute. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Wilma status: general_610_140_screening

Missouris main expungement law is Section 610.140. It can cover arrests, charges, pleas, trials, and convictions, but the offense must be eligible, the waiting period must be complete, all obligations must be satisfied, there must be no pending charges, and the lifetime limits cannot be exceeded.

Missouri expungement generally closes/seals records from ordinary public access. Closed records are not destroyed under the general closure rule; they are inaccessible to the general public but remain available to specified government, criminal justice, licensing, driver-license, public-safety, care-screening, and federal entities. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.120")

In Missouri, expungement usually means the record is closed from public view. It is not always physically destroyed. Some government agencies, law enforcement, licensing bodies, and authorized background-check users may still be able to access it.
Missouri has several different record-clearing routes. Wilma should not treat all Missouri expungements as the same.

For people no longer incarcerated or under supervision, Missouri courts were directed to order expungement of misdemeanor marijuana offenses and qualifying felony marijuana offenses, with exclusions for distribution/delivery to a minor, violence, or marijuana-impaired operation of a motor vehicle. For Class A/B/C felonies and Class D possession of more than three pounds, expungement is ordered upon completion of incarceration, including supervised probation or parole. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")

A marijuana expungement order is legally effective immediately; the person is treated as if never arrested, convicted, or sentenced for the offense; the conviction and sentence are vacated as legally invalid; and the court must issue a certificate of expungement. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")

A 610.140 order closes records held by entities possessing records subject to the order, and the central repository must request that the FBI expunge records from its files. Court records in municipal, associate, or circuit court become confidential and available only to parties or by court order for good cause. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

The order restores civil rights that were restricted because of the criminal record, including the right to vote, hold public office, and serve as a juror. The statute also says an expunged crime may still be considered as a prior offense for sentencing if the person is later found guilty of another offense. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
A person with a 610.140 expungement may generally answer

to an employers question about whether they have been arrested, charged, or convicted if they have no public record of a crime, but must disclose expunged convictions for certain legally restricted jobs or applications. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

Mandatory disclosure situations include applications for Missouri professional licenses, certain gaming/firearm-related licenses or permits, employment with law enforcement/emergency services, federally insured banks/credit unions, insurance businesses subject to federal law, or employers required by law to exclude applicants with certain convictions. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

After a Missouri expungement, many ordinary employers should not see the public record, and you may often answer no to ordinary criminal-history questions. But there are exceptions for courts, later criminal cases, professional licensing, law enforcement, emergency services, banking, insurance, and jobs where federal or state law requires exclusion.

610.140 arrest-only route A petition to expunge records related to an arrest for an eligible crime may be filed no earlier than 18 months from the date of arrest, if during that time the petitioner has not been charged and has not been found guilty of any misdemeanor or felony. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140") Wilma status labels:possible_610_140_arrest_only_18_monthsnot_eligible_yet_arrest_18_month_waitneeds_arrest_date_and_charge_status Important distinction: This is separate from 610.122 false-information arrest expungement and separate from 610.105 automatic closure of certain terminated cases.

610.105 closed-record outcomes If a person was arrested and charged, but the case was later nolle prossed, dismissed, resulted in not guilty, or resulted in suspended imposition of sentence in the prosecuting court, official records pertaining to the case become closed records after final termination, subject to statutory exceptions. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.105")

Some Missouri cases may already be closed by operation of law after dismissal, nolle prosequi, not guilty, or SIS final termination. That is different from a full expungement petition, so we should check what still appears on public Case.net, court records, and the MSHP record.
Wilma status labels:possible_already_closed_610_105needs_public_record_checkneeds_mshp_record_check

A 610.140 order closes records held by entities possessing records subject to the order, and the central repository must request that the FBI expunge records from its files. Court records in municipal, associate, or circuit court become confidential and available only to parties or by court order for good cause. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

The order restores civil rights that were restricted because of the criminal record, including the right to vote, hold public office, and serve as a juror. The statute also says an expunged crime may still be considered as a prior offense for sentencing if the person is later found guilty of another offense. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
A person with a 610.140 expungement may generally answer

to an employers question about whether they have been arrested, charged, or convicted if they have no public record of a crime, but must disclose expunged convictions for certain legally restricted jobs or applications. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

Mandatory disclosure situations include applications for Missouri professional licenses, certain gaming/firearm-related licenses or permits, employment with law enforcement/emergency services, federally insured banks/credit unions, insurance businesses subject to federal law, or employers required by law to exclude applicants with certain convictions. ruleClauses: Missouris adult-use marijuana amendment created marijuana-specific expungement. Article XIV, 2 legalizes personal-use possession and conduct for adults 21+ within limits, including possession of three ounces or less of dried marijuana or equivalent and registered home cultivation within plant limits. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")

A person currently incarcerated in prison, jail, or halfway house may petition the sentencing court to vacate the sentence, order release/supervision relief, and expunge all government records if the marijuana offense would no longer be an offense, would be a lesser offense, or fits listed misdemeanor/Class E/Class D marijuana categories involving possession of three pounds or less, excluding distribution/delivery to a minor, violent offenses, and marijuana-related motor-vehicle intoxication offenses. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")

A person on probation or parole for a qualifying marijuana law violation had a constitutional pathway for automatic vacation of sentence, termination of supervision, and expungement, again subject to exclusions such as distribution/delivery to a minor and operating a motor vehicle while under the influence of marijuana. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")

A 610.140 order closes records held by entities possessing records subject to the order, and the central repository must request that the FBI expunge records from its files. The order restores civil rights that were restricted because of the criminal record, including the right to vote, hold public office, and serve as a juror. sourceSections.title: Missouris core expungement model
sourceSections.text:
Missouri expungement generally closes/seals records from ordinary public access. Closed records are not destroyed under the general closure rule; they are inaccessible to the general public but remain available to specified government, criminal justice, licensing, driver-license, public-safety, care-screening, and federal entities. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.120")
Wilma plain-language script:
In Missouri, expungement usually means the record is closed from public view. It is not always physically destroyed. Some government agencies, law enforcement, licensing bodies, and authorized background-check users may still be able to access it.

Missouri has several different record-clearing routes. Wilma should not treat all Missouri expungements as the same.

Pathway Main authority Best used for
General arrest/charge/plea/trial/conviction expungement 610.140 Most eligible Missouri crimes, including many convictions
False-information / no-probable-cause arrest expungement 610.122-610.123 Arrest-only records based on false information or certain qualifying dismissed/not-guilty moving-violation arrests
Closed-record outcome 610.105 Cases charged but later nolle prossed, dismissed, not guilty, or SIS after final termination
First intoxication-related traffic/boating offense 610.130 First misdemeanor/ordinance DWI/BWI-type offense after 10 years
Marijuana expungement Missouri Constitution Art. XIV, 2 Qualifying marijuana convictions/arrests, including automatic and petition-based relief
Stolen/mistaken identity 610.145 Wrong person named because of identity theft or mistaken identity
Minor in possession of alcohol 311.326 First-time MIP alcohol offense after statutory waiting rule

sourceSections.title: 610.140 excluded offenses
sourceSections.text:
Wilma must screen for statutory exclusions before giving any
likely eligible
result.
The following are not eligible under 610.140:
1 Class A felonies.
2 Dangerous felonies as defined in 556.061.
3 Offenses requiring sex-offender registration.
4 Felony offenses where death is an element.
5 Felony assault, misdemeanor or felony domestic assault, and felony kidnapping.
6 Chapter 566 sex offenses and many specifically listed offenses in other chapters.
7 Offenses eligible under 610.130.
8 Intoxication-related traffic or boating offenses, or operating aircraft with excessive BAC/while intoxicated.
9 Ordinance violations substantially equivalent to excluded offenses.
10 Motor-vehicle-law or ordinance violations committed by a person with, or required to have, a CDL.
11 Most unlawful-use-of-weapons offenses under 571.030, with specific statutory exceptions. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

Wilma script:
Missouri is broad, but not universal. Some serious offenses, sex-registration offenses, domestic assault, felony assault, intoxication-related driving/boating offenses, CDL-related motor vehicle violations, and certain weapon offenses are blocked or need attorney review.

5. Lifetime limits under 610.140
A person can receive more than one expungement under 610.140, but the total lifetime number of crimes expunged under this section cannot exceed:

Category Lifetime limit
Misdemeanor offenses or ordinance violations with authorized imprisonment 3
Felony offenses 2
Infractions No numerical limit
If multiple crimes were committed as part of the same course of criminal conduct, they may be included together and count only as the highest level for current/future eligibility purposes. (

"https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

sourceSections.title: Filing process under 610.140
sourceSections.text:
Where to file
File in the Missouri court where the person was charged or found guilty. For an arrest-only 610.140 petition, the petition is filed in a court of competent jurisdiction in the county where the person was arrested. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Who must be named
The petition must name as defendants all agencies/entities the petitioner believes may possess records, including law enforcement agencies, courts, prosecutors/circuit attorneys, central repositories, and others. The courts order does notaffect an entity that was not named as a defendant. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Wilma must emphasize this. This is a Missouri-specific packet-quality issue.
Wilma script:
In Missouri, naming the right agencies matters. If an agency that has your record is not named in the case, the expungement order may not affect that agencys copy.

Required petition information
The 610.140 petition must include:
Full name
Sex
Race
Drivers license number, if applicable
Current address
Each crime to be expunged
Approximate date charged for each crime
County and municipality where charged
Case number and court name for each crime ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Notice and objection
The clerk gives notice to the prosecuting attorney. If the prosecutor objects, the objection must be written and filed within 30 days after receipt of service. Unless otherwise agreed, the court holds a hearing within 60 days after a written objection is filed. If no objection is filed, the court may still set a hearing and give notice to entities named in the petition. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Court deadline
If the court determines the petitioner meets the criteria, it enters an expungement order. In all 610.140 cases, the court must issue an order of expungement or dismissal within six months of the petition filing. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")

sourceSections.title: Effect of 610.140 expungement
sourceSections.text:
A 610.140 order closes records held by entities possessing records subject to the order, and the central repository must request that the FBI expunge records from its files. Court records in municipal, associate, or circuit court become confidential and available only to parties or by court order for good cause. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
The order restores civil rights that were restricted because of the criminal record, including the right to vote, hold public office, and serve as a juror. The statute also says an expunged crime may still be considered as a prior offense for sentencing if the person is later found guilty of another offense. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
A person with a 610.140 expungement may generally answer
no
to an employers question about whether they have been arrested, charged, or convicted if they have no public record of a crime, but must disclose expunged convictions for certain legally restricted jobs or applications. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Mandatory disclosure situations include applications for Missouri professional licenses, certain gaming/firearm-related licenses or permits, employment with law enforcement/emergency services, federally insured banks/credit unions, insurance businesses subject to federal law, or employers required by law to exclude applicants with certain convictions. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Wilma script:
After a Missouri expungement, many ordinary employers should not see the public record, and you may often answer no to ordinary criminal-history questions. But there are exceptions for courts, later criminal cases, professional licensing, law enforcement, emergency services, banking, insurance, and jobs where federal or state law requires exclusion.

8. 610.140 arrest-only route
A petition to expunge records related to an arrest for an eligible crime may be filed no earlier than 18 months from the date of arrest, if during that time the petitioner has not been charged and has not been found guilty of any misdemeanor or felony. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.140")
Wilma status labels:possible_610_140_arrest_only_18_monthsnot_eligible_yet_arrest_18_month_waitneeds_arrest_date_and_charge_status
Important distinction: This is separate from 610.122 false-information arrest expungement and separate from 610.105 automatic closure of certain terminated cases.

9. 610.105 closed-record outcomes
If a person was arrested and charged, but the case was later nolle prossed, dismissed, resulted in not guilty, or resulted in suspended imposition of sentence in the prosecuting court, official records pertaining to the case become closed records after final termination, subject to statutory exceptions. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.105")
Wilma script:
Some Missouri cases may already be closed by operation of law after dismissal, nolle prosequi, not guilty, or SIS final termination. That is different from a full expungement petition, so we should check what still appears on public Case.net, court records, and the MSHP record.

sourceSections.title: 610.122 / 610.123 arrest expungement
sourceSections.text:
Eligibility
A Missouri arrest record may be expunged under 610.122 if:
1 The arrest was based on false information, and:
there is no probable cause at the time of expungement to believe the person committed the offense;
no charges will be pursued; and
the person did not receive SIS for the arrest offense or related offense; or
2 The person was arrested for or later charged with a misdemeanor Chapter 303 offense or moving violation, excluding intoxication-related traffic offenses, and every related offense/violation was nolle prossed, dismissed, or found not guilty, and the person was not a CDL holder and was not operating a commercial motor vehicle at the time. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.122")
No civil action may be pending relating to the arrest or the records sought to be expunged. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.122")
Procedure
The petition is filed in the civil division of the circuit court in the county of arrest. It must include identity details, offense charged, arrest date, county/municipality, arresting agency, case number/court, and a fingerprint card. The petition must name all agencies/entities believed to possess the records. The court sets a hearing no sooner than 30 days from filing and provides notice. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.123")
Effect
Records ordered expunged under 610.123 are destroyed, blacked out if destruction is not feasible, removed from Missouri electronic files, and the central repository requests FBI expungement. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.124")
Wilma status labels:possible_610_122_false_information_arrestpossible_610_122_moving_violation_no_convictionnot_eligible_610_122_civil_action_pendingneeds_fingerprint_card_610_123

11. 610.130 first intoxication-related traffic/boating offense
A person may apply after at least 10 years to expunge a first intoxication-related traffic or boating offense if it was a misdemeanor or county/city ordinance violation, was not a commercial-motor-vehicle DUI conviction, and the person has not had another intoxication-related traffic/boating conviction since. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.130")
The court must grant if, after hearing, the person has no later intoxication-related traffic/boating convictions, no other later alcohol-related enforcement contacts, and no pending intoxication-related traffic/boating offenses or alcohol-related enforcement actions. A person is entitled to only one expungement under this section, and the statute does not apply to CDL holders or people required to have a CDL. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.130")
Wilma status labels:possible_first_dwi_bwi_expungement_610_130not_eligible_yet_dwi_10_year_waitexcluded_cdl_or_commercial_vehiclelegal_review_intoxication_related_offense
Wilma script:
Missouris regular 610.140 expungement excludes intoxication-related traffic and boating offenses, but Missouri has a separate first-offense DWI/BWI-type pathway under 610.130 after 10 years.

sourceSections.title: Marijuana expungement under Missouri Constitution Article XIV
sourceSections.text:
Missouris adult-use marijuana amendment created marijuana-specific expungement. Article XIV, 2 legalizes personal-use possession and conduct for adults 21+ within limits, including possession of three ounces or less of dried marijuana or equivalent and registered home cultivation within plant limits. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")
Current incarceration
A person currently incarcerated in prison, jail, or halfway house may petition the sentencing court to vacate the sentence, order release/supervision relief, and expunge all government records if the marijuana offense would no longer be an offense, would be a lesser offense, or fits listed misdemeanor/Class E/Class D marijuana categories involving possession of three pounds or less, excluding distribution/delivery to a minor, violent offenses, and marijuana-related motor-vehicle intoxication offenses. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")
Probation/parole
A person on probation or parole for a qualifying marijuana law violation had a constitutional pathway for automatic vacation of sentence, termination of supervision, and expungement, again subject to exclusions such as distribution/delivery to a minor and operating a motor vehicle while under the influence of marijuana. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")
Completed sentences / automatic expungement
For people no longer incarcerated or under supervision, Missouri courts were directed to order expungement of misdemeanor marijuana offenses and qualifying felony marijuana offenses, with exclusions for distribution/delivery to a minor, violence, or marijuana-impaired operation of a motor vehicle. For Class A/B/C felonies and Class D possession of more than three pounds, expungement is ordered upon completion of incarceration, including supervised probation or parole. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")
Effect
A marijuana expungement order is legally effective immediately; the person is treated as if never arrested, convicted, or sentenced for the offense; the conviction and sentence are vacated as legally invalid; and the court must issue a certificate of expungement. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?constit=y&section=XIV+++2")

sourceSections.title: 610.145 stolen or mistaken identity
sourceSections.text:
If a person was named in a charge because another person used their identifying information or because of mistaken identity, and the charges were dismissed or the person was found not guilty, the named person may apply in the court where the charge was last pending for expungement of entries related to apprehension, charge, or trial. The court gives notice to the prosecutor, holds a hearing, and if it finds identity theft or mistaken identity plus dismissal/not guilty, it orders expungement. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.145")
If a charge is dismissed because of another persons use of identifying information or mistaken identity, the prosecutor or judicial officer who ordered dismissal must notify the court, and the court must order expungement of official records. Costs are not taxed against the eligible person. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=610.145")
Wilma status labels:possible_identity_theft_record_610_145possible_mistaken_identity_record_610_145fee_exempt_identity_mistake_route

14. 311.326 minor in possession of alcohol
A person with a first-time violation of Missouris minor-in-possession alcohol statute may apply after at least one year after reaching age 21, if they have not had any other alcohol-related offense. The statute excludes CDL/commercial-motor-vehicle situations, allows only one expungement under this section, and the effect is to restore the person to the status occupied before the arrest, plea, trial, or conviction. ("https://revisor.mo.gov/main/OneSection.aspx?section=311.326")
